caveats: a: No source reports a wage-weighted AI-exposed task share for this six-digit industry or the narrowed contract-manufacturing lens.
a: The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio of 0.3117 covers the broader NAICS population and may not match the labor intensity of private-label and toll manufacturers.
rho: Evidence identifies feasible applications and constraints, not realized adoption rates for the target population.
rho: Implementation will vary sharply between simple nonregulated polishes and EPA-registered public-health products.
e: No denominator study measures the contract-manufacturing share of LMM firms in NAICS 325612.
e: The provided firm count of 125 is estimated from size distributions for the full code; multi-establishment parents and diversified chemical firms can distort the eligible denominator.
s5: Published transactions are selected examples rather than a measured hazard rate.
s5: Ultra-Pak also serves personal-care markets and Formula Corp spans categories adjacent to this NAICS code.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-enabled gross benefit in the narrowed lens.
q: Contract structures differ substantially between commodity filling, proprietary formulations, and regulated antimicrobial programs.
d5: The sources do not isolate outsourced production or hold product quality constant.
d5: Cleaning-product demand can shift among concentrated, refill, reusable, and conventional formats even when underlying sanitation activity is stable.
o: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed displacement rate.
o: The estimate concerns the need for an accountable manufacturing operator, not whether the current supplier or current headcount remains unchanged.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are brands, retailers, distributors, and institutional or industrial product marketers. Revenue recurs through production runs and replenishment orders and is commonly quoted per batch, unit, or packaged program, with formulation, testing, regulatory support, warehousing, and fulfillment bundled or separately charged.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes outsourced manufacturing with proprietary branded production, so the lens is narrowed to contract, toll, and private-label operators whose repeat external-customer revenue makes the service construct coherent. The narrowing is based on business-model comparability, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Proprietary branded-product manufacturers without meaningful outsourced manufacturing revenue; captive plants; distributors; janitorial or application contractors; soap and detergent manufacturing classified in NAICS 325611; personal-care or cosmetic production outside this code; and firms outside the lower-middle-market control-acquisition band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contract, toll, and private-label manufacturers of sanitation and specialty cleaning goods, including disinfectants, sanitizers, polishes, waxes, and institutional or industrial cleaners, that repeatedly formulate, blend, fill, package, document, or fulfill products for external brands and customers.

a.bridge: NIST describes AI applications among small and medium manufacturers rather than measuring a wage-weighted task share for NAICS 325612. I map its documented predictive quality, maintenance, forecasting, and process-optimization use cases, together with the formulation, quality, regulatory, production-planning, and fulfillment workflows shown by contract manufacturers, to the lens's current task mix. This likely overstates substitution where physical batching, filling, sanitation, and material handling dominate, while it may understate avoidable hiring in document-heavy quality and customer-service work.
caveats: No source reports a wage-weighted AI-exposed task share for this six-digit industry or the narrowed contract-manufacturing lens.
The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio of 0.3117 covers the broader NAICS population and may not match the labor intensity of private-label and toll manufacturers.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupational roster from several eligible plants, paired with time-and-motion coding of tasks already automated and a plant-level AI pilot log, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The exposed opportunity is concentrated in production scheduling, demand and inventory forecasting, batch-record review, quality trend detection, preventive maintenance, formula and compliance document retrieval, quoting, and customer administration. Mixing, changeovers, sampling, filling, and warehouse movement remain substantially physical, keeping the base below one-third of wages.

d5.bridge: Stepan reports recent demand changes in laundry, cleaning, and industrial-cleaning end markets, while ACI surveys US consumer attitudes toward cleaning and hygiene; neither measures constant-price year-five demand for outsourced NAICS 325612 production. I treat durable hygiene salience and mixed current cleaning volumes as support for approximately flat-to-modest growth, then remove price effects conceptually. Consumer-attitude evidence may bias the estimate high relative to institutional procurement, while one public supplier's volumes may bias it toward its own product mix.
caveats: The sources do not isolate outsourced production or hold product quality constant.
Cleaning-product demand can shift among concentrated, refill, reusable, and conventional formats even when underlying sanitation activity is stable.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar shipment and physical-volume series for the relevant product classes from Census product statistics, Circana or Nielsen retail data, and ISSA institutional-channel surveys, separated into outsourced and captive production, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Cleaning and hygiene are recurring replenishment needs, industrial-cleaning demand provides support, and brands continue to outsource product development and production. Demand is unlikely to repeat pandemic-era spikes; product concentration, green-chemistry substitution, dilution systems, retailer destocking, and weak commodity-cleaning volumes keep the downside near flat or slightly below current quantity.

caveats: No denominator study measures the contract-manufacturing share of LMM firms in NAICS 325612.
The provided firm count of 125 is estimated from size distributions for the full code; multi-establishment parents and diversified chemical firms can distort the eligible denominator.
e.change_evidence: A complete establishment-to-parent list from Data Axle, PitchBook, or Grata, manually coded from websites and customer or revenue disclosures for outsourced manufacturing, would materially change this share.
e.rationale: Manufacturer examples confirm a real contract, toll, and private-label segment, but the NAICS code also contains branded and captive production. I estimate that roughly two-fifths of LMM-band firms have repeat outsourced manufacturing as a sufficiently material activity to qualify after excluding proprietary-product businesses and nonoperating entities.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325612 ($2,282,845,514) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325612 ($7,808,638,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $450,897 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325612); class <5: 152 firms, avg revenue/firm $497,362 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $89,575 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 76 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,098,408 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $377,923 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,727,070 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,031,445 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 68 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,431,485 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,779,211 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $65,601,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,814,740 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $126,186,212 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,726,137 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 125

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed displacement rate.
The estimate concerns the need for an accountable manufacturing operator, not whether the current supplier or current headcount remains unchanged.
o.change_evidence: A customer survey covering insourcing plans, supplier switching, formulation ownership, concentrated or refill adoption, and five-year sourcing commitments, supplemented by plant-capacity announcements, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: The year-five basket still consists mainly of physical chemical formulation, blending, filling, testing, labeling, packaging, and shipment. Software can improve and coordinate those activities but cannot supply the material product. Some brands may self-source or vertically integrate, and concentrated or refill formats can reduce runs, so the operator-required share is not one.

q.bridge: Stepan is a larger public chemical producer rather than an LMM contract manufacturer, and its raw-material price pass-through concerns input inflation rather than AI savings. I use its reported pass-through behavior, plus contract manufacturers' turnkey service scope, to infer how readily customers can reprice standardized production versus differentiated formulation, compliance, and fulfillment. The bridge likely understates retention for confidential small-batch programs with switching friction and overstates it where retailer procurement rebids commoditized private-label volume.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-enabled gross benefit in the narrowed lens.
Contract structures differ substantially between commodity filling, proprietary formulations, and regulated antimicrobial programs.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and quote histories from several eligible firms, including escalation clauses, open-book provisions, rebid cadence, gross-margin bridges, and customer churn following productivity changes, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Per-unit and per-batch contracts, renewal negotiations, procurement benchmarking, and competition from alternative blenders should return much of a visible labor or yield benefit to customers over five years. Operators retain more where gains are embedded in minimum runs, faster changeovers, lower scrap, better service, proprietary workflow data, or regulatory and formulation support rather than itemized labor charges.

rho.bridge: NIST's implementation lessons cover small and medium manufacturers generally, while EPA sources describe product-registration, label, and efficacy constraints for the antimicrobial subset rather than an observed five-year implementation rate. I translate those cross-industry adoption enablers and this sector's documented compliance burden into the fraction of the exposed task opportunity likely to reach production use. The result may be biased low for clean administrative workflows and high for regulated release decisions that still require validated records and accountable human review.
caveats: Evidence identifies feasible applications and constraints, not realized adoption rates for the target population.
Implementation will vary sharply between simple nonregulated polishes and EPA-registered public-health products.
rho.change_evidence: Structured interviews with plant managers and quality or regulatory leaders at at least ten eligible firms, including budgets, data readiness, validation requirements, pilot conversion rates, and deployed-use metrics, would materially move this range.
rho.rationale: Forecasting, scheduling, document search, maintenance triage, and quality anomaly detection can be introduced incrementally. Implementation is slower where source data are fragmented, formulas and claims are customer-confidential, validation is required, or an EPA-regulated label and efficacy record makes autonomous changes unacceptable.

s5.bridge: The Formula Corp and Ultra-Pak transactions are observed control acquisitions of relevant contract manufacturers, but two deals do not measure a five-year hazard across all eligible firms. I use them as evidence that strategic and lower-middle-market financial buyers can execute qualifying transfers, then bound the probability around a broad private-company ownership-cycle judgment. This can overstate turnover if the published deals are unusually visible and understate it if many small transactions are undisclosed.
caveats: Published transactions are selected examples rather than a measured hazard rate.
Ultra-Pak also serves personal-care markets and Formula Corp spans categories adjacent to this NAICS code.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, and industry advisers, matched to a verified eligible-firm denominator and including undisclosed add-ons, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Recent majority and control acquisitions demonstrate buyer appetite for scalable cleaning-product formulation and packaging platforms. Nevertheless, sparse disclosed deal counts, family ownership, environmental diligence, customer concentration, and owner dependence make a transfer within a fixed five-year window uncertain.

principal_driver: Recurring physical replenishment plus document-heavy formulation, quality, scheduling, and compliance workflows creates a credible, implementable AI opportunity.
principal_weakness: The target population and benefit-retention rate are weakly measured, and commoditized private-label programs expose savings to customer repricing and competitive rebids.

run_meta.title: Polish and Other Sanitation Good Manufacturing

sources.supports: Small and medium manufacturer AI use cases in predictive maintenance, predictive quality, scrap and yield improvement, forecasting, and process optimization, together with implementation lessons.
sources.title: Artificial Intelligence in Manufacturing: Real World Success Stories and Lessons Learned

sources.supports: Existence and workflow scope of a US private-label and contract manufacturer offering formulation, scalable production, automated filling and packaging, quality, regulatory, and compliance services.
sources.title: Formula Corp - Private Label & Contract Manufacturing Partner

sources.supports: EPA-registered toll and contract manufacturing, quality and compliance, flexible batch sizes, blending, packaging, and physical plant capabilities for disinfectants.
sources.title: Toll Manufacturing of EPA Disinfectants

sources.supports: Contract chemical tolling for private and government customers, including confidential proprietary blending, packaging, and delivery.
sources.title: Contract Manufacturing | B&B Blending

sources.supports: A 2025 majority acquisition of a custom cleaning-products manufacturer providing turnkey formulation, blending, filling, labeling, packaging, regulatory, and fulfillment services.
sources.title: San Francisco Equity Partners Acquires Formula Corp, a Leading Developer and Manufacturer of Cleaning Products for Consumer and Professional Use

sources.supports: A 2024 lower-middle-market-backed control acquisition of a regional contract manufacturer and packager serving cleaning-product and janitorial-sanitation markets.
sources.title: Makai Capital-Backed Paladin Holdings Acquires Ultra-Pak, Inc.

sources.supports: Raw-material cost pass-through through selling prices and mixed recent demand, including lower commodity laundry and cleaning volume partly offset by industrial-cleaning demand.
sources.title: Stepan Company 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: US survey evidence that cleaning and hygiene remain closely associated with public health and continue to shape reported cleaning behavior.
sources.title: ACI Survey: 3/4 Americans Are Changing Their Cleaning Habits to Improve Their Health

sources.supports: EPA regulation and registration of disinfectants, approval of label language, and product registration identification, including products sold under multiple company names.
sources.title: Selected EPA-Registered Disinfectants

sources.supports: Efficacy-data requirements and regulated public-health antimicrobial claims, including disinfectant, virucide, sanitizer, sterilant, and tuberculocide claims.
sources.title: Pesticide Registration Manual: Chapter 4 - Additional Considerations for Antimicrobial Products

title: Polish and Other Sanitation Good Manufacturing
